did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used the Exhaustive Chi-Squared Automatic Interaction Detection (Exhaustive CHAID) tree-building algorithm in SPSS to develop a decision-tree for triaging skin lesions using LC-OCT criteria. CHAID is a statistical machine learning method used for classification and prediction.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and a 'retrospective classification study'. It presents original experimental data involving 75 clinically equivocal lesions in organ transplant recipients scanned with LC-OCT, developed a decision-tree using Exhaustive CHAID, and reports specific diagnostic performance metrics (sensitivity, NNB) and patient demographics.

title: Skin lesion triage in organ transplant recipients using line-field confocal optical coherence tomography: a retrospective classification study
brief_summary: This retrospective classification study investigated the potential of line-field confocal optical coherence tomography (LC-OCT) for triaging clinically equivocal skin lesions in organ transplant recipients (OTRs). The researchers analyzed 75 lesions from 51 OTRs using a handheld LC-OCT device and applied the Exhaustive Chi-Squared Automatic Interaction Detection (Exhaustive CHAID) algorithm to develop a 4-step decision-tree based on five specific LC-OCT criteria. The study evaluated the decision-tree's performance against histology-verified diagnoses for squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, Bowen’s disease, actinic keratosis, and benign lesions. Key results showed high sensitivity scores of 87.5% for SCC, 94.4% for BCC, 83.3% for Bowen’s disease, and 72.7% for actinic keratosis. The authors concluded that this LC-OCT decision-tree can assist in real-time clinical decision-making for immunocompromised patients, potentially reducing unnecessary biopsies.

lesion_types_studied.details: Histology-verified malignant lesion; n=8 total (4 moderately differentiated, 4 highly differentiated). Sensitivity 87.5%.
Histology-verified malignant lesion; n=18 total (4 superficial, 13 nodular, 1 infiltrative). Sensitivity 94.4%.

Histology-verified benign lesions; n=15 total. Includes viral warts (n=5), seborrheic keratosis (n=4), dermatitis/folliculitis/inflammation/demodex (n=4), sebaceous adenoma (n=1), and solar elastosis (n=1). Sensitivity 53.3%.

all_task_types.target: Histology-verified diagnostic categories (Squamous cell carcinoma, Basal cell carcinoma, Bowen's disease, Actinic keratosis, Benign lesions)
all_task_types.details: The study developed a 4-step decision-tree using the Exhaustive CHAID algorithm to classify clinically equivocal skin lesions into five discrete histology-verified diagnostic categories. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity for each class. The title explicitly describes it as a 'retrospective classification study'.

datasets_used.name: Organ Transplant Recipient (OTR) Cohort
datasets_used.description: A retrospective cohort of clinically equivocal skin lesions from organ transplant recipients recruited at the Department of Dermatology, Bispebjerg Hospital, Copenhagen, Denmark, between February 2022 and October 2024.

use.training: Used for developing the Exhaustive CHAID decision-tree via 10-fold cross-validation (90% of data used for training in each fold).
use.validation: Used for validation via 10-fold cross-validation (10% of data used for testing in each fold).
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation (sensitivity calculation) against histology-verified diagnoses.

statistical_methods_used.method: Exhaustive Chi-Squared Automatic Interaction Detection (Exhaustive CHAID)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To develop a 4-step decision-tree for triaging skin lesions by identifying the most significant associations between LC-OCT criteria and histology-verified diagnoses.
To increase generalizability by training the algorithm on 90% of the data and testing on 10% of the data, repeated 10 times.
To determine significant associations between LC-OCT criteria and diagnostic categories, using a significance threshold of p < 0.05.
To evaluate the performance of the decision-tree for each lesion type (SCC, BCC, BD, AK, Benign), calculated as true positives divided by (true positives + false negatives).
To provide a frame of reference for the decision-tree's performance, calculated as the mathematical inverse of the positive predictive value.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant association between the presence/absence of specific LC-OCT criteria and the histology-verified diagnostic category of the skin lesion.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant association between the presence/absence of specific LC-OCT criteria and the histology-verified diagnostic category of the skin lesion.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The Exhaustive CHAID algorithm identified LC-OCT criteria with significant associations (p < 0.05) to build the decision-tree. Specific p-values were reported for individual criteria: lobules for BCC (p < 0.001), severe dysplasia for SCC (p = 0.008), broad strands for SCC (p = 0.001), keratin pearls for SCC (p = 0.008), severe dysplasia for BD (p = 0.008), and mild-moderate dysplasia for AK (p = 0.001).

Organ Transplant Recipient (OTR) Cohort.dataset_description: Retrospective cohort of 75 clinically equivocal skin lesions from 51 organ transplant recipients (OTRs) scanned with LC-OCT and verified by histology.
Organ Transplant Recipient (OTR) Cohort.evaluation_setting: 10-fold cross-validation using Exhaustive CHAID algorithm to develop a 4-step decision-tree based on 5 LC-OCT criteria.

Exhaustive CHAID Decision-Tree.Key_Criteria_SCC: Severe dysplasia (p=0.008), broad strands (p=0.001), keratin pearls (p=0.008)
Exhaustive CHAID Decision-Tree.Misclassifications_Upstaged: Sebaceous adenoma misclassified as BCC; two Seborrheic Keratoses (SK) misclassified as SCC
Exhaustive CHAID Decision-Tree.Misclassifications_Downstaged: One BCC false negative (ulcerated surface/challenging angle); one BD false negative (predicted SCC)

Organ Transplant Recipient (OTR) Cohort.notes: The decision-tree triages lesions into three groups: keratinocyte carcinomas (BCC/SCC), premalignant/in-situ lesions (BD/AK), and benign lesions. The study noted that the algorithm tended to err on the side of caution (upstaging) in some cases. LC-OCT criteria excluded from the tree but potentially important: collagen alterations, clefting, tumor budding, and interrupted/well-defined DEJ.

architecture_used.main_model: Exhaustive Chi-Squared Automatic Interaction Detection (Exhaustive CHAID) decision-tree

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Exhaustive Chi-Squared Automatic Interaction Detection (Exhaustive CHAID) decision-tree
preprocessing_techniques: Data recoding: Presence of an LC-OCT criterion was coded as 1 and absence as 0.
Data consolidation: Multiple images per lesion (n = 1–3) were consolidated into a single record (n = 1) to ensure all identified criteria were retained.

Logical constraint application: Dataset was recoded assuming mild/moderate and severe dysplasia cannot coexist within a single image, and likewise a well-defined and an interrupted dermal–epidermal junction (DEJ). In cases of co-occurrence, the higher severity grade was retained (e.g., severe dysplasia over mild/moderate dysplasia, interrupted DEJ over well-defined DEJ).
conclusions: The study designed an LC-OCT decision-tree for use in immunocompromised patients to assist in the triage of equivocal skin lesions with potential for improving clinical decision-making.

The developed 4-step decision-tree, based on five LC-OCT criteria (severe dysplasia, broad strands, keratin pearls for SCC; lobules for BCC; severe or mild-moderate dysplasia for premalignant lesions), achieved high sensitivity for keratinocyte carcinomas and premalignant lesions: 87.5% for SCC, 94.4% for BCC, 83.3% for Bowen’s disease, and 72.7% for actinic keratosis.
The decision-tree triages lesions into three groups: keratinocyte carcinomas (BCC/SCC), premalignant/in-situ lesions (BD/AK), and benign lesions.

The algorithm tended to err on the side of caution, occasionally upstaging benign lesions (e.g., sebaceous adenoma misclassified as BCC, seborrheic keratoses misclassified as SCC), which may help avoid missing aggressive cancers in high-risk patients.

The study suggests that LC-OCT triage could potentially reduce unnecessary biopsies in organ transplant recipients, although further refinement incorporating specific LC-OCT criteria for benign lesions is needed to improve specificity.
important_remarks: Small sample size: The study included only 75 lesions from 51 patients, with very low counts for some classes (e.g., n=8 for SCC), limiting statistical power and generalizability.
Single-center retrospective design: Data was collected from a single specialized transplant clinic in Copenhagen, Denmark, between 2022 and 2024, introducing potential selection bias.
Lack of external validation: The authors explicitly state that the algorithm should be validated in an external cohort to determine its performance in a different sample.
Confirmatory bias: Image assessment was conducted by a single evaluator, although scans were acquired by the same experienced clinician to reduce observer variability.
High false negative rate for benign lesions: Sensitivity for benign lesions was only 53.3% (7 false negatives), indicating the tree struggles to correctly identify benign conditions.

Tendency to upstage: The algorithm tended to err on the side of caution, upstaging benign lesions (e.g., sebaceous adenoma misclassified as BCC, seborrheic keratoses as SCC), which may reduce unnecessary biopsies but lowers specificity.

Exclusion of potentially important criteria: The CHAID algorithm excluded five LC-OCT criteria (collagen alterations, clefting, tumor budding, interrupted/well-defined DEJ) that may still be clinically relevant for skin cancer assessment.
Technical limitations in specific anatomical sites: A false negative BCC case was attributed to a challenging lesion location (alar crease) and ulcerated surface, which limited full LC-OCT coverage.
No consensus on clinical thresholds: The study notes there is currently no consensus on whether the observed Number Needed to Biopsy (NNB) thresholds are clinically acceptable for implementation.
LC-OCT should not stand alone: The authors recommend that LC-OCT examination should complement, not replace, clinical inspection and dermoscopy assessments.
